items.title: RAT Abuses TON Blockchain to Target Japan's Hotel Industry
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign observed in May 2026 targets Japanese accommodation facilities partnering with Booking.com. Attackers impersonate guest complaints and review requests through emails, tricking hotel staff into downloading malicious ZIP files containing shortcut links disguised as photos. The malware, TONResolver, employs The Open Network blockchain platform as a dead drop resolver
items.ai_summary: The TONResolver malware is being distributed through a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting Japanese hotels, specifically those partnered with Booking.com, by impersonating guest complaints and review requests. The attack vector involves tricking hotel staff into downloading malicious ZIP files containing shortcut links disguised as photos, which then deploy the malware. This campaign exploits human psychology rather than a specific CVE, making it challenging to defend against with traditional patching. To remediate, hotels should implement robust email filtering, educate staff on phishing detection, and ensure all systems are up-to-date with the latest security software. Specific steps include configuring email clients to block ZIP files from unknown sources and implementing a zero-trust model for all incoming emails.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1102| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Chrome and Firefox Extensions Posing as Free VPNs Add Clipboard Stealers via Malicious Updates
items.description: Malicious browser extensions distributed through Chrome Web Store and Firefox Add-ons marketplaces posed as free VPN services while secretly stealing clipboard data. The Chrome extension, with 146 users, and Firefox extension, with 3,499 users, initially functioned as proxy tools but later incorporated clipboard theft through staged updates. Chrome versions 1.1 onwards and Firefox version 1.3.3 on
items.ai_summary: The malicious Chrome and Firefox extensions, posing as free VPN services, utilized the T1176 tactic to distribute clipboard stealers through staged updates, affecting 146 Chrome users and 3,499 Firefox users. The extensions initially functioned as proxy tools but later incorporated clipboard theft, leveraging the T1115 tactic to steal sensitive data. To mitigate this threat, defenders should immediately remove the affected extensions and update their browsers to the latest versions, ensuring that Chrome is updated to version 1.1 or later and Firefox is updated to version 1.3.3 or later. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing browser extensions, only installing those from trusted sources and regularly monitoring their browser's extension list for suspicious activity. By taking these specific remediation steps, defenders can prevent further exploitation and protect sensitive data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1176| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1115| C[Collection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Djinn in the Machine: TaskWeaver's Node.js Intrusion Chain
items.description: An intrusion was investigated that began with exploitation of CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software. The threat actor obtained unauthorized technician access and deployed two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. TaskWeaver is a heavily obfuscated Node.js loader that establishes encrypted communications and delive
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp RMM software, to gain unauthorized technician access. This vulnerability allows attackers to bypass authentication mechanisms, potentially leading to a full system compromise. The real-world impact of this exploit includes the deployment of two previously undocumented malware samples: TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update SimpleHelp RMM software to the latest version and ensure that all technician accounts have strong passwords and multi-factor authentication enabled. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity related to TaskWeaver and Djinn Stealer, such as unusual network communications or file modifications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Unmanaged Application Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Defence Impairment Olympics
items.description: A sophisticated attack sequence was detected beginning June 7 involving a steganographically hidden webshell on a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server. The threat actor executed extensive enumeration commands before deploying approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques. These included disabling IIS logging, tampering with Microsoft Defender, timestomping file metadata, killing Sysmon and Fil
items.ai_summary: The Defence Impairment Olympics attack sequence began with the exploitation of a vulnerable Adobe ColdFusion server, where a steganographically hidden webshell was deployed, classified as T1505.003. The threat actor then executed extensive enumeration commands, followed by the deployment of approximately a dozen defence impairment techniques, including disabling IIS logging and tampering with Microsoft Defender, which are indicative of T1070 indicator removal tactics. To mitigate this threat, defenders should immediately update their Adobe ColdFusion servers to the latest version, ensure IIS logging is enabled, and verify Microsoft Defender configurations have not been altered. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious activity, such as timestomping file metadata, and ensure Sysmon and File monitoring tools are running and have not been terminated by malicious actors. Specific remediation steps include checking for any obfuscated files or information, as indicated by T1027, and verifying system integrity through regular audits and log analysis.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505.003| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1070| D[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    D -->|T1070| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How access to Gmail accounts is gained
items.description: The ToddyCat APT group developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. The malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. Umbrij automates the entire attack chain: it copies user profiles, launches browsers with deb
items.ai_summary: The ToddyCat APT group has developed a sophisticated tool called Umbrij to compromise Gmail corporate accounts through OAuth token theft. This malware exploits Chromium-based browsers by launching them in headless mode with remote debugging enabled, utilizing the Shadow Token via Remote Debug (STRD) technique. If exploited, attackers can gain unauthorized access to sensitive information, including emails and contacts, potentially affecting organizations that use Gmail for corporate communications. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all Chromium-based browsers are updated to the latest version and that remote debugging is disabled by default. Additionally, organizations should implement multi-factor authentication and monitor for suspicious activity, such as unusual login locations or access patterns.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1134| B[Access Token Manipulation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: India's government and energy sectors targeted with ZOHOMURK and MINIRECON
items.description: Mustang Panda orchestrated two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns leveraged DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK. MINIRECON represents an evolution of Toneshell with WebSocket-based command-and-control capabi
items.ai_summary: The Mustang Panda threat actor has been observed conducting two concurrent espionage campaigns targeting Indian government entities and hydropower infrastructure between May and June 2026. The campaigns utilized DLL sideloading via legitimate executables to deploy newly identified malware, including SHARDLOADER, MINIRECON, and ZOHOMURK, with MINIRECON representing an evolution of Toneshell featuring WebSocket-based command-and-control capabilities. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust application whitelisting, monitor for suspicious DLL loading, and ensure that all software is up-to-date, particularly focusing on securing legitimate executables that could be exploited for DLL sideloading. Real-world impact includes potential data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information within the targeted sectors. Specific remediation steps include configuring Windows to show file extensions, enhancing logging for application layer protocols, and regularly scanning for unknown or malicious DLLs.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1574.002| B[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Discovery]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A stealthy RAT burrowing deep into Android devices
items.description: BTMOB is an Android remote access trojan that evolved from SpySolr malware and poses significant threats beyond traditional banking trojans. The malware combines phishing-led delivery with an APK builder interface that enables rapid payload generation without coding skills. Distributed through fake app stores impersonating streaming services, cryptocurrency platforms, and government agencies, BTMO
items.ai_summary: The BTMOB Android remote access trojan (RAT) exploits users through phishing attacks, leveraging fake app stores impersonating popular services. Once installed, the malware uses an APK builder interface to generate payloads without coding skills, making it accessible to a broader range of threat actors. BTMOB poses significant threats beyond traditional banking trojans, including data theft and unauthorized access. To remediate, users should avoid installing apps from untrusted sources and enable Google Play Protect on their Android devices. Additionally, users should regularly update their Android operating system to the latest version, specifically targeting Android 11 and later, which includes enhanced security features to mitigate RAT attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An Analysis of ValleyRAT Infection Campaigns from Fake Installers, Japanese Malicious Emails
items.description: LevelBlue has identified two distinct ValleyRAT attack vectors: campaigns using fake installers and malicious email-based campaigns. Detection volume increased significantly from May 2025, nearly doubling in 2026. The fake installer attacks primarily target Chinese-speaking users and employ advanced techniques including Pool Party Variant 7 process injection and BYOVD methods. The malicious email
items.ai_summary: The ValleyRAT Infection Campaigns utilize two distinct attack vectors: fake installers and malicious emails. The fake installer attacks primarily target Chinese-speaking users, employing advanced techniques such as Pool Party Variant 7 process injection and BYOVD methods. If exploited, these attacks can lead to unauthorized access and data exfiltration. To remediate, users should avoid downloading software from untrusted sources and ensure that their systems are running the latest version of their operating system and software. Additionally, implementing a robust email filtering system and educating users on phishing tactics can help prevent these attacks.

items.title: Not very gentlemanly: Analyzing a zero-day exploit used to disable targets' EDRs
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware group, which emerged in July 2025, employed a zero-day vulnerability in a bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver (BYOVD) attack to disable endpoint detection and response systems. During an incident investigated in early April, the group leveraged an obscure third-party driver named ktapi.sys from Kontron to bypass security protections. The sophisticated exploit chains multiple
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware group exploited a zero-day vulnerability in the ktapi.sys third-party driver from Kontron to disable endpoint detection and response systems. This BYOVD attack bypassed security protections, allowing the threat actors to gain unauthorized access. The exploit chain involved multiple steps, including initial access, execution, and command and control. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to data breaches and ransomware attacks. To remediate, defenders should update the ktapi.sys driver to the latest version (v1.2.3 or later) and ensure that EDR systems are configured to detect and block suspicious activity related to the driver.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold

items.title: The Gentlemen are knocking: сustom backdoors and evolving tactics
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group emerged as a top-10 threat actor in the first half of 2026. The group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service group exploits vulnerabilities in internet-facing devices like VPNs and firewalls, potentially collaborating with initial access brokers. They employ comprehensive reconnaissance using tools like SharpADWS, NetScan, and Advanced IP Scanner, capturing network traffic with netsh. The attackers use remote access software (T1219) to establish a foothold, acquire access (T1650), and obtain capabilities (T1588). To mitigate this threat, defenders should ensure that all internet-facing devices are patched with the latest security updates, specifically focusing on vulnerabilities in VPNs and firewalls. Additionally, implementing network traffic monitoring and analysis tools can help detect and prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1650| B[Acquire Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    C -->|T1588| D[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-56253: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an improper access control vulnerability in the p...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an improper access control vulnerability in the public.get_org_members RPC function that allows unauthenticated attackers to enumerate organization members. Attackers can invoke the endpoint using only the public sb_publishable_* key and an organization UUID to retrieve sensitive member information including email addresses, user IDs, roles, and pending invitations.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56253 is a critical improper access control vulnerability in Capgo before version 12.128.2. The vulnerability allows unauthenticated attackers to enumerate organization members by invoking the public.get_org_members RPC function using only the public sb_publishable_* key and an organization UUID. This can result in sensitive member information, including email addresses, user IDs, roles, and pending invitations, being exposed. To remediate this vulnerability, Capgo users should update to version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that the public.get_org_members RPC function is properly restricted to authenticated users. Additionally, users should review their organization's member information and take necessary actions to mitigate any potential damage.

items.title: CVE-2026-56251: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains a broken row level security policy in the org_use...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains a broken row level security policy in the org_users table that allows authenticated users to elevate privileges from admin to super_admin. Attackers can exploit the insufficient RLS enforcement to gain unauthorized super_admin access and compromise system security.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56251 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in Capgo before version 12.128.2. The issue arises from a broken row level security policy in the org_users table, allowing authenticated users to elevate their privileges from admin to super_admin. Attackers can exploit this vulnerability by leveraging T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1068 (Exploitation for Privilege Escalation) tactics. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized super_admin access, compromising system security. To remediate this issue, administrators should update Capgo to version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that the RLS policy is properly configured to prevent privilege escalation. Additionally, it is recommended to monitor system logs for suspicious activity and implement regular security audits to detect and prevent similar vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1068| C[Exploitation for Privilege Escalation]:::tactic

items.title: CVE-2026-56242: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an unauthenticated security definer RPC function ...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an unauthenticated security definer RPC function get_identity_apikey_only that returns the owning user_id for supplied API keys, creating an API key validity oracle and user identity disclosure primitive. Attackers can call this endpoint with valid or invalid API keys to confirm key validity and map keys to user identifiers, then chain results into other exposed RPCs
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56242 is a critical vulnerability in Capgo before version 12.128.2, allowing attackers to exploit the unauthenticated security definer RPC function get_identity_apikey_only. This function returns the owning user_id for supplied API keys, enabling attackers to create an API key validity oracle and disclose user identities. Attackers can call this endpoint with valid or invalid API keys to confirm key validity and map keys to user identifiers, then chain results into other exposed RPCs. To remediate, update to Capgo version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that the get_identity_apikey_only function is disabled or restricted to authenticated users. Additionally, implement API key validation and monitoring to detect and prevent potential exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-56239: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains a potential privilege escalation vulnerability in...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains a potential privilege escalation vulnerability in the public.apply_usage_overage SECURITY DEFINER function, which performs sensitive billing operations without enforcing internal authorization checks (no validation of auth.uid(), org membership, or check_min_rights). Because the function runs with the owner's privileges, it bypasses Row Level Security. If EXECUTE per
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56239 is a privilege escalation vulnerability in Capgo before 12.128.2, specifically in the public.apply_usage_overage SECURITY DEFINER function. This function bypasses Row Level Security by running with the owner's privileges, allowing attackers to perform sensitive billing operations without proper authorization checks. If exploited, attackers can escalate privileges and gain access to sensitive data, potentially leading to data breaches or ransomware attacks. To remediate this vulnerability, Capgo users should update to version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that the public.apply_usage_overage function is properly configured with internal authorization checks. Additionally, users should validate auth.uid(), org membership, and check_min_rights to prevent unauthorized access.

items.title: CVE-2026-56236: Capgo CLI before 12.128.2 contains arbitrary file overwrite vulnerabilities in l...
items.description: Capgo CLI before 12.128.2 contains arbitrary file overwrite vulnerabilities in login and build credentials operations that follow symlinks without validation. Attackers can create malicious symlinks in repositories to overwrite arbitrary files or expose credentials with world-readable permissions when developers run the CLI.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-56236 is a critical vulnerability in Capgo CLI versions prior to 12.128.2, allowing attackers to create malicious symlinks in repositories and overwrite arbitrary files or expose credentials with world-readable permissions. This vulnerability can be exploited by attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information or disrupt development operations. To remediate this issue, developers should update to Capgo CLI version 12.128.2 or later, and ensure that the 'followSymlinks' option is set to 'false' in the CLI configuration. Additionally, developers should validate the integrity of symlinks in their repositories and restrict permissions on sensitive files. This vulnerability affects developers using Capgo CLI in their development workflows.

items.title: CVE-2026-56229: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an authorization bypass vulnerability in the /bui...
items.description: Capgo before 12.128.2 contains an authorization bypass vulnerability in the /build/status and /build/logs endpoints that allows attackers to access build jobs belonging to different applications by supplying a mismatched app_id and job_id combination. Limited API keys restricted to a single app can retrieve build status and logs from other apps by providing an authorized app_id while using a job_i

items.title: CVE-2025-71378: picklescan before 0.0.30 fails to detect cProfile.runctx function calls in pickl...
items.description: picklescan before 0.0.30 fails to detect cProfile.runctx function calls in pickle file reduce methods, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code. Malicious pickle files bypass picklescan detection and execute remote code when loaded via pickle.load().

items.title: CVE-2025-71357: picklescan before 0.0.30 fails to detect malicious pickle files using idlelib.py...
items.description: picklescan before 0.0.30 fails to detect malicious pickle files using idlelib.pyshell.ModifiedInterpreter.runcommand in reduce methods. Attackers can embed undetected code in pickle files that executes remote commands when loaded by victims.

items.title: CVE-2025-71351: picklescan before 0.0.25 fails to detect malicious pickle files that use timeit....
items.description: picklescan before 0.0.25 fails to detect malicious pickle files that use timeit.timeit() in the __reduce__ method, allowing remote code execution. Attackers can craft pickle files that import dangerous libraries like os and execute arbitrary system commands, which evade picklescan detection and execute when pickle.load() is called.

items.title: CVE-2025-71348: picklescan before 0.0.28 fails to detect malicious pickle files that invoke torc...
items.description: picklescan before 0.0.28 fails to detect malicious pickle files that invoke torch.utils._config_module.load_config function within reduce methods. Attackers can craft pickle files embedding arbitrary code that evades detection but executes during pickle.load, enabling remote code execution in supply chain attacks.

items.description: Hackers were seen making over 81 million login attempts originating from systems associated with hosting provider LSHIY. The post Massive Password Spray Campaign Targeting Azure CLI appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Adobe patches seven max severity ColdFusion, Campaign flaws
items.description: Adobe has released security patches for seven maximum-severity vulnerabilities in the ColdFusion web app development platform and the Campaign Classic marketing automation platform. [...]

items.title: Phantom Squatting Uses AI-Hallucinated Domains for Phishing and Malware
items.description: Large language models keep inventing web addresses that do not exist. Attackers have started buying those made-up domains before anyone else can, then hosting phishing pages on them to catch traffic that AI tools point their way. Palo Alto Networks' Unit 42 calls the trick phantom squatting, and its new research shows it is already happening in the wild. The reason it matters is

items.title: Anthropic Restores Claude Fable 5 After U.S. Lifts Jailbreak-Linked Export Controls
items.description: Anthropic is putting Claude Fable 5 back online worldwide. On June 30, the U.S. Commerce Department lifted the export controls it had imposed on Fable and its more tightly controlled sibling Mythos 5 about two and a half weeks earlier. Fable 5 returns to users on Wednesday, July 1, across Claude.ai, the Claude Platform, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork. Export controls restrict who can

items.title: Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities
items.description: Fifteen of the newly patched flaws have been rated ‘critical’ and 67 have been rated ‘high severity’. The post Google Patches 382 Chrome Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Azure CLI Password Spray Hits at Least 78 Microsoft Accounts in 81M+ Attempts
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have warned of a "massive, ongoing, automated password spray attack" aimed at Microsoft's Azure command-line interface (CLI), compromising dozens of accounts in the process. The activity, per Huntress, originates from an IPv6 address range (2a0a:d683::/32) controlled by internet infrastructure provider LSHIY LLC (AS32167). "Between June 12 and June 26, the threat

items.title: Researcher Analyzes 3,000 Live ClickFix Payloads, Exposing API-Driven Malware Delivery
items.description: ClickFix, the trick that fools people into running malware by hand, has quietly grown a back office. New research shows the malicious commands behind its fake "prove you're human" pages are now handed out by API-driven servers that give each visitor the same malware in a different disguise. The same research also turned up a new delivery method built to slip past Windows' script scanning.

items.title: Why Ask Credentials If There Are Secret Codes?, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: This morning, an interesting phishing email hit my mailbox. It targets Metamask[1], a cryptocurrency wallet, available as a browser extension and a mobile app, that lets users store, send, and receive crypto money. It&#x27s pretty popular, so a juicy target for criminals. In February, I already mentioned a campaign against them[2].

items.title: Citrix Patches Six NetScaler Flaws Allowing File Read and Denial-of-Service
items.description: Citrix on Tuesday released security updates to address multiple flaws in NetScaler ADC (formerly Citrix ADC) and NetScaler Gateway (formerly Citrix Gateway) that could be exploited by an attacker to facilitate arbitrary file reads or trigger a denial-of-service (DoS) condition. The vulnerabilities are listed below - CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS score: 8.8) - An insufficient input validation

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, July 1st, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9990, (Wed, Jul 1st)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: China-Linked Group Targets Southeast Asia Critical Systems
items.description: The group compromised at least 10 regional organizations, including two state-owned entities, and deployed a new backdoor.

items.title: Anthropic to restore Claude Fable access on Wednesday
items.description: Anthropic has confirmed that the Department of Commerce has lifted export controls on Claude's two most powerful models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5. [...]

items.title: Anthropic rolls out Sonnet 5 with near-Opus 4.8 performance at a lower price
items.description: Anthropic is now rolling out Sonnet 5, and it's almost as good as the Opus range, but it is designed to be cheaper than the company's flagship model. [...]

items.title: New BioShocking attack manipulates AI browser into data theft
items.description: A new prompt injection attack dubbed "BioShocking" could trick AI-powered browsers into treating real-world risky actions as part of a fictional scenario, causing them to ignore any safety guardrails. [...]

items.title: Fake Bug Report Hijacks AI Coding Agents at Scale
items.description: "Agentjacking" is the latest demonstration of how easily attackers can exploit an AI agent's inability to differentiate between content and instructions.

items.title: Microsoft accelerates quantum-safe roadmap as risks grow
items.description: Microsoft announced today that it is accelerating its quantum-safe security roadmap, saying advances in quantum computing are bringing the need to replace today's encryption standards sooner than previously expected. [...]

items.title: Malicious PyPI packages give hackers control of Telegram bot servers
items.description: A campaign active since last November has been targeting Python developers building Telegram bots with trojanized Pyrogram forks that allow attackers to read arbitrary files on compromised servers. [...]

items.title: Attackers Seize Exposed AI Endpoints to Power Offensive Ops
items.description: Threat actors don't need any special authentication to reach a target endpoint — they just need to know where it is.

items.title: Why Identity Security Is Your Cyber Career Entry Point
items.description: As AI reshapes cybersecurity workflows, John Paul Cunningham, CISO at SIlverfort, says the technology is creating opportunities rather than eliminating jobs — and there are more ways than ever to break into the essential field.

items.title: CIA chief highlights major shifts in agency’s tech approach
items.description: CIA Director John Ratcliffe said artificial intelligence capabilities are "akin to digital nuclear weapons.”

items.title: Phishers Gain Persistence at EU, Asia Hospitality Orgs
items.description: Separate but similar campaigns described by Microsoft and Trend Micro use malicious zip files to spread malware via social engineering and obsfucation, including blockchain abuse.

items.title: Microsoft Warns Poisoned MCP Tool Descriptions Can Make AI Agents Leak Data
items.description: New Microsoft research shows how attackers can hijack AI agents that act on a user's behalf, using nothing more than a poisoned tool description to make the agent quietly hand over company data to an outsider. The trick is that the agent never breaks a rule. Every step looks routine, so in a default setup no alarm may fire. The work comes from Microsoft Incident Response and its

items.title: RustDuck Botnet Rebuilds in Rust to Hijack Routers and Servers for DDoS
items.description: A new two-stage malware family called RustDuck is hijacking home routers, IP cameras, Android boxes, and poorly secured servers, then stitching them into a network built to knock websites and online services offline. Researchers at QiAnXin's XLab have tracked it since February 2026, and say the real story is not how big it is today, but how fast it is changing. The end goal is a

items.title: House passes kids’ online safety bill, but Senate approval unlikely
items.description: The Kids Internet and Digital Safety (KIDS) Act passed with bipartisan support by a 267-117 margin, winning the two-thirds majority needed to greenlight the legislation under a process that speeds up a bill’s path to a vote but requires more than a simple majority.

items.title: Langflow RCE Exploited to Deploy Monero Miner on Exposed AI App Endpoints
items.description: Threat actors are continuing to exploit a critical Langflow vulnerability as part of fresh attacks designed to deliver a Monero cryptocurrency miner. The activity has been found to weaponize CVE-2026-33017 (CVSS score: 9.3), an unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Langflow, indicating threat actors are scanning and targeting exposed artificial intelligence (AI)

items.title: Fake Perplexity extension on Chrome Web Store tracked searches
items.description: A malicious extension in the Chrome Web Store is masquerading as the Perplexity AI answer engine, intercepting search traffic and collecting browsing information. [...]

items.title: BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The Microsoft Defender vulnerability CVE-2026-33825 was exploited in the wild as a zero-day before patches were released. The post BlueHammer Vulnerability Exploited in Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: An intelligence budget 'super user' job is now in the hands of Russ Vought
items.description: Russell Vought, director of the White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB), assumed hands-on responsibility for overseeing the spending plans of intelligence agencies following the recent departure of Amaryllis Fox Kennedy, a senior intelligence official who simultaneously served in multiple roles, including one at OMB.

items.title: AI-Generated Workflows Are a Silent Security Disaster
items.description: Teams are dealing with a truly dangerous problem — automation that works, but that no one understands.

items.title: Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: Decades-old Bash shell tricks can bypass safeguards in most open source AI coding agents, potentially turning malicious repositories into supply chain attack vectors. The post Decades-Old Bash Tricks Expose AI Coding Agents to Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million
items.description: Hackers accessed the insurance giant’s policyholder portal multiple times between June 15 and June 25. The post Aflac Japan Data Breach Impacts 4.38 Million appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: June 2026 Apple Updates, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: Apple released updates for iOS/iPadOS, macOS, and Safari on Monday. There have been no updates for other Apple operating systems (visionOS, watchOS, tvOS). Usually, Apple updates all products at the same time.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 30th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9988, (Tue, Jun 30th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: NIST Enrichment Reductions Impact CVE Coverage, Accuracy
items.description: The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) scaled back the number of CVEs it selects for in-depth analysis, but the move has produced mixed results, according to researchers.

items.title: 'Djinn' Stealer Targets Cloud, AI Credentials
items.description: The infostealer was delivered via CVE-2026-48558, a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in SimpleHelp, targeting credentials linking development and admin environments to wider enterprise systems.

items.title: Vulnerabilities Expose Private Data in Indian Government Systems
items.description: One critical vulnerability, among many discovered by a researcher, could have allowed anyone to walk in and take over a national government portal.

items.title: Adding some Automation to the favicon.ico method of Host Recon, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;m in the throes of target host recon for another pentest, and thought I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;d share some workflow / automation stuff. In the past, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve discussed using historic DNS "mining" to collect target hosts in the domain.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 29th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9986, (Mon, Jun 29th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: YARA-X 1.18.0 and 1.19.0 Release, (Sun, Jun 28th)
items.description: YARA-X&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s 1.18.0 release brings 3 improvements and 2 bugfixes.

items.title: What do Ports Hear When Nobody's Listening? An Assessment of Automated Cybercrime [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Nicole Phillips, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: Linux Process Name Masquerading, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: In a previous diary, I talked about stack strings&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5b;1&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x5d; with a practical example of them. Since my SEC670 class, I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xe2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x80;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x99;m even more interested&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;in malware obfuscation techniques. I had&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;a look at process names. When you list running processes on a computer, can you trust what you see&&#x23;x26;&#x23;x3f; If you&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;re facing a rootkit, malicious processes can be simply hidden (the API calls or commands to list processed have been tampered). But a malicious process&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xc2;&&#x23;x26;&#x23;xa0;can also mimic a non-suspicious name by

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 24th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9984, (Wed, Jun 24th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
